definition: Bootstrapping is the practice of building and growing a company using only the founders' own resources and revenue generated by the business, without taking external investment. A bootstrapped company keeps initial costs low, reinvests revenue, and grows organically. The primary advantage is that founders retain 100% ownership and full control over decisions. The trade-off is slower growth and personal financial risk, since the founders are putting their own money (and often their time without salary) on the line. Many successful startups bootstrap through the early stages before raising outside capital once they have proven product-market fit.
formalDefinition: Bootstrapping is a self-financing strategy in which a business is capitalized through the founders' personal resources, sweat equity, and internally generated cash flows rather than external debt or equity financing. It may include personal savings, credit facilities, pre-sales revenue, and reinvested profits. Bootstrapping is contrasted with externally funded models (angel, venture capital, or debt-financed startups).
whenYouSeeThis: Bootstrapping is discussed in the context of startup funding strategy, term sheets, and investor presentations. While there are no specific legal documents for bootstrapping, the absence of external investors simplifies the corporate structure: no preferred share classes, no investor rights agreements, and no board observer seats. The founders' agreement and any co-founder equity split are the key documents.
founderNote: Bootstrapping gives you maximum control but limits your speed. It works best when your product does not require large upfront capital expenditure and you can reach revenue quickly. Even if you plan to raise venture capital eventually, bootstrapping to a proof of concept or first sales dramatically improves your negotiating position: you will give up less equity at a higher valuation. Canadian government grants (IRAP, BCIP, Mitacs) are a non-dilutive funding source that pairs well with bootstrapping since they do not require giving up equity.
